title: John Baptist Wu

infobox.value: Cardinal-Priest of [Santa Maria del Monte Carmelo a Mostacciano](/wiki/Santa_Maria_del_Monte_Carmelo_a_Mostacciano "Santa Maria del Monte Carmelo a Mostacciano") (1988–2002)
29 June 1988 by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II")

23 September 2002(2002-09-23) (aged 77)  [Queen Mary Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Mary_Hospital_(Hong_Kong) "Queen Mary Hospital (Hong Kong)"), [Pok Fu Lam](/wiki/Pok_Fu_Lam "Pok Fu Lam"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong")

St. Michael's Cemetery, [Happy Valley](/wiki/Happy_Valley,_Hong_Kong "Happy Valley, Hong Kong"), Hong Kong (until 2022)[[1]](#cite_note-1) Chapel of St. Anthony, Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception, [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") (from 2022)
[John Baptist Wu Cheng-chung's coat of arms](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_John_Baptist_Wu_Cheng-chung.svg "John Baptist Wu Cheng-chung's coat of arms")
**[Bishop of Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_Roman_Catholic_bishops_of_Hong_Kong "List of Roman Catholic bishops of Hong Kong")*

([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 胡振中; [Cantonese Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese"): *Wu Cheng-chung*; 26 March 1925 – 23 September 2002) was the fifth [Roman Catholic bishop of Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_Roman_Catholic_bishops_of_Hong_Kong "List of Roman Catholic bishops of Hong Kong") and the first [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") from that diocese. He was a member of the [Congregation for the Evangelization of Peoples](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Evangelization_of_Peoples "Congregation for the Evangelization of Peoples"), the Pontifical Council for Social Communications and the [Congregation for Divine Worship and the Discipline of the Sacraments](/wiki/Congregation_for_Divine_Worship_and_the_Discipline_of_the_Sacraments "Congregation for Divine Worship and the Discipline of the Sacraments").

Su, Xinqi (11 January 2019). ["Top officials join Hong Kong Catholics in packed cathedral for Bishop Michael Yeung's funeral mass"](https://www.scmp.com/news/hong-kong/society/article/2181639/top-officials-join-hong-kong-catholics-packed-cathedral). *South China Morning Post*. Retrieved 16 January 2019.
Wu, John (October 1989). "March into the Bright Decade: Pastoral Exhortation of Cardinal John B. Wu on the Pastoral Commitment of the Catholic Diocese of Hong Kong". *Catholic Information Service*.

**John Baptist Wu Cheng-chung** ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 胡振中; [Cantonese Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese"): *Wu Cheng-chung*; 26 March 1925 – 23 September 2002) was the fifth [Roman Catholic bishop of Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_Roman_Catholic_bishops_of_Hong_Kong "List of Roman Catholic bishops of Hong Kong") and the first [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholic_Church) "Cardinal (Catholic Church)") from that diocese. He was a member of the [Congregation for the Evangelization of Peoples](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Evangelization_of_Peoples "Congregation for the Evangelization of Peoples"), the Pontifical Council for Social Communications and the [Congregation for Divine Worship and the Discipline of the Sacraments](/wiki/Congregation_for_Divine_Worship_and_the_Discipline_of_the_Sacraments "Congregation for Divine Worship and the Discipline of the Sacraments").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Baptist_Wu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

A Hakka, Wu was born in the village of Ho Hau, Wu-hua (Province of [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), [Diocese of Kai-ying](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Kai-ying&action=edit&redlink=1 "Diocese of Kai-ying (page does not exist)")). Baptized in the village's parish church, he received his primary education there. He joined the diocesan minor seminary of Ka-ying for his secondary education in 1940 and was ordained in 1952.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

He was appointed the fifth Bishop of the Hong Kong Catholic Diocese by [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") as successor to Bishop [Peter Lei Wang-kei](/wiki/Peter_Lei "Peter Lei") who had died on 23 July 1974. He arrived in Hong Kong and on 25 July was consecrated and installed as Bishop of the Hong Kong in the [Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_in_Hong_Kong "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception in Hong Kong") in 1975.

After due preparation, he initiated a Diocesan Renewal Movement for priests, laity and religious. On 25 March 1985, he led a five-member delegation on a seven-day visit to Beijing and Shanghai, at the invitation of the National Bureau of Religious Affairs under the State Council of China. He was the first [bishop of Hong Kong](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Hong_Kong "Roman Catholic Diocese of Hong Kong") to visit the [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"). Later on 21 January 1986, he led a seven-member delegation on a ten-day visit to [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") and the eastern part of his home Province, [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), at the invitation of the Bureau of Religious Affairs of that Province. This visit marked the first reunion with his 85-year-old mother, after a separation of 40 years.

On 29 May 1988, Wu was named a Cardinal by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II").[[3]](#cite_note-:10-3): 228 He was the first Cardinal from Hong Kong.[[3]](#cite_note-:10-3): 228

After the [Tiananmen Square protests of 1989](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square_protests_of_1989 "Tiananmen Square protests of 1989"), on 11 July 1989, he wrote a letter to all the bishops in the world, requesting them to appeal for justice, order and democracy in China.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On 1 September 1991, Wu issued a pastoral letter that exhorting the faithful to give full support to the direct elections to the Legislative Council, the first time in the history of Hong Kong, on 15 September.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) In 1999, he convoked the "Diocesan Synod" to meet the pastoral needs of the Third Millennium.

He died of cancer and diabetes on 23 September 2002 at [Queen Mary Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Mary_Hospital_(Hong_Kong) "Queen Mary Hospital (Hong Kong)") in Hong Kong, aged 77. He was buried in [St. Michael's Catholic Cemetery](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Catholic_Cemetery "St. Michael's Catholic Cemetery"), Happy Valley.

On September 2022, twenty years after his death, Wu's remains were exhumed and re-interred in the crypt of the [Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Hong_Kong) "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Hong Kong)").
